# Unofficial Meeting Notes
## Conroe ISD School Safety and Security Committee
### Regular Meeting — November 30, 2023

> **Note:** These are unofficial notes based on an auto-generated transcript of the meeting. They are not official minutes and may contain errors or omissions.

---

**Meeting Called to Order:** 4:01 PM
**Adjourned:** 5:15 PM (following closed session)

**Next Meeting:** Spring 2024 (March or April — to be announced)

---

## Item 1: Approval of Minutes

- Minutes from the previous meeting were emailed out in advance.
- No corrections or changes were raised.
- **Motion to approve:** Ethan (last name not stated)
- **Second:** Chief (last name not stated)
- **Vote:** Approved unanimously by show of hands.

---

## Item 2: Staying Safe School Safety Response Training (Texas School Safety Center)

**Presenters:** Captain Hook (referenced), Chief Blakelock

**Background:**
- The Texas School Safety Center developed a curriculum called "Staying Safe" — a school safety response training focused on alert/run/hide/fight concepts (avoid, deny, defend) aimed at **students**.
- The district currently uses the CRASE (Civilian Response to Active Shooter Events) model to train **staff and community members**, but has not taught a similar curriculum directly to students.
- Committee approval was required before district personnel could attend the train-the-trainer session.

**Key Discussion Points:**
- The training would be a **6-hour train-the-trainer session** held at a regional center (Huntsville).
- At least three staff members would need to attend (tiered for high school, junior high, and elementary implementation).
- **Attending the training does not commit the district to implementing the program.** Multiple districts have declined the program after review.
- Chief Blakelock emphasized the importance of ensuring that if students are trained, it does not undermine teachers' ability to control and direct students during an emergency.
- Concerns were raised about **overtraining students at secondary campuses** where threats are typically internal, potentially giving bad actors insight into response protocols.
- No students would attend the training — this is for adult district staff only.

**Motion:** Dr. N. (last name unclear) moved to approve sending staff to the training.
**Second:** Received (name unclear)
**Vote:** Approved unanimously by show of hands.

---

## Item 3: Bond Funding for Districtwide Safety and Security

**Presenters:** Mr. McCord, Mr. Foster, Dr. Winkler

### Overview of Funding Sources

**Bond Funds:**
- **$35 million** allocated for safety and security in the November 2023 bond referendum.
- Covers approximately 75 campuses over four years.
- **Key limitation:** Bond funds **cannot** be used for personnel/salary — only capital improvements.
- Reminder that even $35 million will not cover everything; decisions on priorities are necessary.

**State Grant — School Safety Standards Grant:**
- Awarded **$2.8 million** (spring 2023) — used to encapsulate **27 elementary schools** with 6-foot black galvanized slatted fencing and made progress on 3 additional schools.

**State Grant — SAFE Grant Cycle 1:**
- Awarded **$10.1 million** (recently announced).
- **Planned uses:**
  - Install **impact-resistant film** on all exterior glass doors and immediate adjacent windows district-wide (all schools) — achieving full **Chapter 61 compliance** well ahead of the August 2024 deadline.
  - Remaining funds to complete 6-foot slatted fencing at all remaining elementary and intermediate schools.
- **SAFE Grant Cycle 2** expected in spring 2024 (formulaic, non-competitive); district hoping for approximately $1 million.

### Chapter 61 Compliance (Commissioner's Rules — School Facilities)
- Effective August 2024, all exterior perimeter doors with glass inserts and immediately adjacent windows must have either impact-resistant film or be behind a secure enclosed area.
- The fencing creates anti-scaling enclosed areas (not full perimeter — typically back/side of schools).
- Egress gates are required for emergency exit.

**Impact-Resistant Film Testing:**
- Before demolishing an end-of-life building at Conroe High School, the district applied the film and had officers test it with metal bars, rocks, and other tools.
- Officers found it **virtually impossible** to breach in a timely manner — described as phenomenal in delaying unauthorized entry.

### Proposed Uses of Bond Funds (Mr. Foster)

| Category | Details | Estimated Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Control (Exterior)** | ~20 additional card readers per campus to eliminate physical keys from exterior doors | ~$5,000 per door opening |
| **Parking Lot Gates** | Pipe gates to secure parking lots during emergencies/weekends | ~$5,000–$6,000 per driveway |
| **Security Camera Lifecycle Replacement** | Replace aging cameras, servers (~$18–20K each), and components | ~$25,000–$30,000/building for partial; ~$200,000/elementary for full system upgrade |
| **Secure Corridors (New Concept)** | Retrofit hallways to create secured grade-level "pods" at elementary/intermediate schools, allowing doors to open within secured zones | ~$15,000 per opening; 6–20 openings per campus depending on layout |
| **Safety & Communications Equipment** | Police radios (~$5,000 each), in-car radios (~$5,000), radio repeaters (~$1/sq ft), lifecycle replacements | Varies; repeater system ~$500,000+ for large campus |

**Note:** If all items were pursued across all campuses, costs would **exceed $35 million**.

### Secure Corridors Discussion (Dr. Winkler)
- Current locked-door policy creates **isolation** for teachers and students, reduces instructional time (lost minutes unlocking/relocking doors), and limits collaboration during intervention periods.
- New elementary prototype features secured "pods" by grade level with keycarded entry points, interior bathrooms, and visibility.
- Some existing campuses (e.g., Ford Elementary, Haley Elementary) could be **retrofitted** with this concept relatively easily.
- Benefits: Added security layer, improved community/collaboration, better temperature regulation, increased instructional time.

### AI-Based Weapons Detection Discussion

**Raised by:** Jason (committee member)

- Described **Zero Eyes** and similar AI weapons detection software that overlays on existing camera systems.
- Technology detects weapons via AI, then a **human reviewer** confirms before triggering alerts (~3-second detection).
- Cost: ~$100 per camera (subscription-based); estimated ~$150,000/year per high school campus.
- District has approximately **15,000 cameras** currently used primarily for recording/deterrent — not actively monitored in real-time at most campuses.
- Multiple vendors exist (Zero Eyes, 21st Century AI, and 12+ others).
- **Bond funds cannot be used for software subscriptions** — would need to come from operating budget or potentially grant funds.
- Committee expressed strong interest in exploring further.

**Action Items (No Formal Votes — Direction Given):**
- Mr. Foster received direction to proceed with planning for:
  - 100% exterior door access control on new builds and retrofits
  - Secure corridor retrofits at applicable campuses
  - Continuing camera lifecycle replacements
  - Parking lot gates
  - Communications equipment lifecycle management
- District will explore AI weapons detection vendors through proper procurement/RFP process; potential subcommittee may be formed.
- Staff to investigate whether grant funds could offset subscription-based costs.

---

## Item 4: Fentanyl Awareness Update

**Presenters:** Miss Robertson, Miss Rodriguez

**Actions Taken:**
- Health Services and Guidance & Counseling websites contain substance use information for families.
- Developed **"One Pill Kills" information cards** using TEA toolkit — distributed to high schools, junior highs, and intermediate schools.
- Collaborated with the **American Heart Association** on an informational flyer distributed to all school nurses.
- **Narcan (naloxone) expanded to ALL Conroe ISD campuses** this school year (previously only junior highs and high schools since January 2023).
- October **social media campaign** using TEA toolkit shared district-wide.
- **"Hidden in Plain Sight"** community presentation on substance use warning signs and prevention.

**Key Context:**
- Narcan has been used on **at least two occasions** in the district.
- Dr. N. emphasized fentanyl is a significant real-world issue and praised the team for being proactive ahead of any legislative mandate.
- Compared Narcan deployment to the district's AED program, which has had **four successful life-saving deployments** in district history, including a recent incident at an elementary school where a student with no prior medical history went into distress and was revived through CPR and AED use. The student was taken to ICU and recovered.

---

## Item 5: Rave 911 Implementation Update

**Presenters:** Captain Hook (referenced), Chief Blakelock

**Status:** Fully implemented district-wide.

**Key Details:**
- All teachers and substitutes can download and access the Rave 911 app.
- All district facilities are **geofenced** within the system.
- **Over 5,000 employees** have fully completed the app download (approximately 50%+ of staff).
- The system has been **used effectively multiple times** since implementation — including for athletic injuries at football games, demonstrating improved communication.
- Successfully tested at three different campuses with three different alert types (including active assailant), coordinated with 911 dispatch.

**Concerns Raised (by Jason/911 perspective):**
- A **rash of false alarms** is occurring across the region (not at CISD) — including two false active assailant button presses at other districts/private schools.
- Employees should be discouraged from testing the system without coordination; a test mode can be activated by notifying 911 dispatch.
- CISD's training approach appears to be working — no false alarm incidents from CISD.

**Phase 2 — In Progress:**
- Working with 911 and outside law enforcement to extend active assailant alerts to **external law enforcement agencies** within the geographic area, enabling faster multi-agency response.
